Good morning, Ibrahim,

I hope you’re having a good week.

I wanted to provide you with a brief weekly update. To date, we have not heard from opposing counsel. They still have until the 17th of this month to respond to our demands, and at this point, I have not followed up, as we are allowing the full demand period to run.

Accordingly, we are still within the 14-day demand window, which does not expire until the 17th.

Separately, I did happen to drive by one of their facilities and observed signage stating “We Love LA,” which I found notable given the circumstances. That said, our focus remains on allowing the demand period to conclude and proceeding strategically based on their response—or lack thereof.

Please feel free to reach out if you have any questions. I’ll continue to keep you informed as things develop.

Best regards,

Good morning, Aaliyah,

I hope you are doing well.

I wanted to provide you with a brief status update on your matter. At this point, we are likely to proceed either with mediation or a formal demand, depending on how the next step develops.

I am currently waiting to hear back from Mr. Lopez regarding the citation. Once that information is received and reviewed, we will be in a position to move forward accordingly.

In the meantime, please feel free to reach out to me if anything has changed at work, if any new issues have emerged, or if you have additional information that you believe is relevant. Otherwise, we will allow this process to play out and proceed once the outstanding item is resolved.

As always, I will keep you updated as things progress.

Best regards,

Good morning, Mr. Sesay,

I hope you are doing well. I wanted to provide you with a brief status update on your case. As of today, it has been seven (7) days since my last conversation with the insurance adjuster, who indicated that he would need approximately seven days to review the claims.

I will be sending a follow-up email to the adjuster today to check on the status of that review. In the meantime, I wanted to place you on notice of where things currently stand.

Until we receive a response, there is nothing further required from you at this time. As always, if anything has changed on your end or if you have any questions, please do not hesitate to reach out.

I will keep you updated as soon as I hear back.

Best regards,

Work-Kaiser Permanente-Panorama City
Role: Medical Assistant in the Pulmonary Department
Full Time
Duration: 26 Years
Union: SEIU 399
Date of Incident: 11/05/2025 Time: 3:00-3:30 pm
Place of Incident: 13651 Willard Street, Panorama City, 91402 (Building No 5, Lower Level)
Incident occurred in the Pulmonary Department (Lower Level)  
Occurred where the injured party usually works
3 Witnesses: 
-Respiratory Therapist James DaCumos
-Humberto Gonzalez
-Rosalva Mendoza

Injured says she was going into the procedure room to prepare things for the following day, when she perceived/sensed a strange, acidic smell. She says the smell was very strong, acidic, and it burned her throat as she inhaled.

Next, she recalls coughing very violently. The injured person says that a few minutes after coughing, she started to experience shortness of breath. She tried to manage the symptoms with her own inhaler at home. However, once home, she discovered that the symptoms worsened and could not be managed at home, she was taken to the hospital by her daughter. 

Upon arrival at the emergency room (Kaiser Panorama City), she was examined, and it was discovered that the irritant or chemical was the underlying cause of the extreme pulmonary flare-up. They placed her on a Bypap so they could avoid intubating her. She was then admitted to the DOU floor, which is a step down from the ICU.

Injured was hospitalized from 11/05/2025 to 11/08/2025. While hospitalized, she received a higher level of corticosteroids and magnesium. She was also given her usual medication and antibiotics.

Worker's Compensation denied her claim due to uncooperation, and they don't believe it was a true Workers' Comp Issue.

-She has been under the care of a Worker's Comp doctor and her own Pulmonologist.
-Denied for 02/2/2026, but the injured was not notified; she only found out 22 days later when she reached out to EDD.
-Has been on State Disability

Post Discharge Care

Pre-Existing Medical Conditions:
1. Chronic Asthma
2. Has been on Oxygen during strenuous activity. Since the incident, she has been on oxygen 24 hours.
3. Diabetes Type II Insulin Dependent
4. Fibromyalgia
5. Acid Reflux
6. Limited Mobility-Can not walk more than 20 feet without shortness of breath. Husband recently purchased her a scooter.

The injured last day of work was 11/5/2025. 

Chemical Emanated from the Elevator Machine Room. She came to this conclusion based on the smell. She became exposed as the fumes moved/spread towards her. The elevator was being fixed as she was going towards the department. No signage, fans, or warning that there could be possible exposure.

The company working on the elevator were outside contractors. 
Insured says this is the second time such exposure has occured and they refuse to disclose the chemical that was being released. No tests were done of the cite based on her knowledge.

Children
26 year old Son
23 year old Daughter

No Grandchildren

She cares for her 87 year old mother who resides with her.

column8: Meet and Confer Email to Bryan and Peter
column9:
Counsel,

I recently left messages for both of you regarding this matter and am following up in writing.

I understand that correspondence inviting a meet and confer was sent to Plaintiff shortly before my substitution of attorney was filed. Now that I am counsel of record, please direct all future communications to my office. I would have reached out sooner had that correspondence come through me directly.

In the meantime, our review of the records has continued to develop. In addition to the tracheostomy and airway management issues, the documentation reflects significant pressure ulcers and circumstances under which Plaintiff was not permitted to visit her husband despite his deteriorating condition. These facts materially affect our evaluation of exposure.

Before either side incurs further motion and discovery expenses, I would welcome a discussion regarding a potential resolution. Please also confirm the available liability coverage for the relevant period, including any primary and excess layers, pursuant to CCP 2017.210.

I look forward to your response.

Best regards,
Victoria Tulsidas, Esq.

Dear Mr. Koonce,

I hope you are well.

I write regarding the DMG Wellness matter. After further discussion with our client, I have confirmed they are prepared to extend a $10,000 settlement offer to resolve this matter in its entirety.

While our client continues to deny the allegations and maintains that the claims lack merit, this offer is being made in the interest of avoiding the time and expense associated with continued litigation. As you can appreciate, further proceedings will likely result in increased attorneys’ fees and costs on both sides, with no guarantee of a materially different outcome.

Additionally, without waiving any defenses, I believe it is important to be candid that our client has already sustained significant financial losses in connection with this underlying investment and is not in a position to meaningfully expand this offer. As such, this proposal reflects a practical effort to bring finality to the dispute rather than an assessment of liability.

We remain open to discussing a reasonable resolution and are hopeful that the parties can reach an agreement without the need for court intervention.

Please let me know your thoughts.

Best regards,
